cards.text: The throw statement syntax is: {{c1::throw}} expression;. The expression can be any type, but is typically an {{c2::exception class object}}.

cards.question: What types can be thrown as exceptions in this code?
cards.code:
throw 42;              // int
throw 3.14;            // double
throw "Error!";        // const char*
throw std::string("Error");  // std::string
throw MyException();   // custom class
cards.answer: Any type can be thrown: primitives (int, double), pointers (const char*), standard library objects (std::string), or custom classes.
cards.explanation: C++ allows throwing any type as an exception, though using exception class objects is considered best practice.

cards.text: A {{c1::try}} block is used to detect exceptions thrown within its scope. It must be followed by one or more {{c2::catch}} blocks.
A catch block begins with {{c1::catch}} followed by a parameter declaration in parentheses, which specifies the {{c2::exception type}} to catch.

cards.question: What's the best way to write the catch parameter for a custom exception class?
cards.code:
catch (MyException e)           // ?
catch (MyException& e)          // ?
catch (const MyException& e)    // ?
cards.answer: catch (const MyException& e) is best - it avoids copying and prevents modification.
cards.explanation: Using const reference avoids expensive copying of exception objects and prevents accidental modification, which is generally unnecessary.

cards.front: Can a try block have multiple catch blocks?
cards.back: Yes, a try block can have multiple catch blocks to handle different exception types. The catch blocks are checked in order from top to bottom.

cards.question: Which catch block executes if divide() throws an int?
cards.answer: The second catch block (catch (int code)) executes.
cards.explanation: Catch blocks are matched by type. Only the first matching catch block is executed.

cards.text: A catch-all handler {{c1::catch (...)}} will catch exceptions of {{c2::any type}}. It should be placed {{c3::last}} in the catch block sequence.

cards.front: What happens if an exception is thrown but not caught by any catch block?
cards.back: The program calls std::terminate(), which by default calls std::abort() and terminates the program abnormally.

cards.question: What is the output of this code?
cards.explanation: Code after a throw statement in the same block is never executed. After the catch block, execution continues normally.

cards.concept: How exception handling works in C++
cards.explanation:
Exception handling follows these steps:
  • throw statement raises an exception
  • Program immediately exits the current function
  • Searches for matching catch block up the call stack
  • First matching catch block handles the exception
  • Execution continues after the catch block
  • If no match found, std::terminate() is called

cards.text: When catching exception objects, prefer {{c1::const reference}} (const Type&) to avoid {{c2::copying}} and prevent {{c3::modification}} of the exception.
